title: Dow Jones| Nasdaq |S&P 500| US Stock Market Today | Live: Brent crude rises back above $80 per barrel amid conflict concerns; stocks retreat - The Economic Times
text:
Dow Jones| Nasdaq |S&P 500| US Stock Market Live: U.S. stocks declined after Trump said the Iran deal was over and hinted at fresh strikes, lifting oil prices and risk aversion. Dow fell nearly 1%, while gains in Broadcom and chip stocks limited losses. Investors now await Fed minutes for policy cues.

Pakistan called on all parties to uphold their commitments under the Islamabad Memorandum of Understanding, hours after US President Donald Trump said the interim deal with Iran was “over.”

In a statement, Pakistan’s foreign ministry said the MoU remains a key framework for mutual respect, regional stability and shared prosperity.

The 14-point agreement, reached last month between the US and Iran, aimed to halt hostilities and ensure the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz.

Bank of America has lowered its 2026 average gold price forecast by 14% to $4,360 an ounce, citing expectations of a more hawkish Federal Reserve. The revision comes amid pressure on gold from stronger rate hike bets, a firm dollar and elevated oil prices.

Despite the near-term downgrade, BofA still sees gold reaching $5,000 an ounce once the Fed’s tightening cycle ends. JPMorgan also flagged downside risks in the short term due to potential early rate hikes, while keeping a positive longer-term outlook.

Other major banks remain broadly bullish on gold, with several projecting prices between $5,000 and $6,000 per ounce by 2026 or beyond, reflecting expectations of sustained demand and easing monetary conditions over time.

Brent crude, the global oil benchmark, surged back above $80 per barrel on Wednesday after US President Donald Trump said the ceasefire with Iran was over.

Brent crude futures for September delivery jumped 8% to $80.12 a barrel at around 1520 GMT, while US benchmark West Texas Intermediate (WTI) crude for August delivery rose 7.7% to $75.83 a barrel.

- Matthew Ryan, head of market strategy at Ebury

An IMF official said global inflation expectations remain broadly well-anchored, though some signs of upward drift are emerging in certain regions.

U.S. President Donald Trump signalled a hardening stance on Iran, declaring that the existing truce is effectively finished despite ongoing diplomatic efforts. Speaking at the NATO summit, he cast doubt on the viability of further negotiations, even as talks continue in the background.

"As far as I'm concerned, it's over," Trump said.

"I'll let our wonderful negotiators keep talking if they want, but I don't see it," he added.
